title: The Body Keeps the Score — But What Does That Actually Mean
researcherTopic: meditation and neuroscience, the illusion of the self
metaDescription: The phrase 'the body keeps the score' is well-known, but what does it mean for healing trauma? Learn how the nervous system stores memory and how to read its signals.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, intersecting lines of a neural network made of shimmering gold thread. The background is soft and out of focus, creating a sense of warmth, complexity, and inner space.
htmlContent:
The scent of rain on hot asphalt, a specific key in a minor chord, the way the late afternoon sun slants through a window ... these are not just sensory inputs. The mind might register a pleasant smell or a familiar song, but the body registers a story, a threat, a reason to constrict, to brace, to prepare for an impact that happened decades ago and is happening again right now in the silent, cellular theater of its own tissues.

The Echo in the Tissues

The phrase "the body keeps the score" has become so ubiquitous that it risks losing its earth-shattering meaning, smoothed into a palatable platitude. But its reality is anything but smooth. It doesn’t mean the body is a simple ledger, tallying up wrongs. When an experience is too much for the cognitive, narrative-making mind to process - when the thinking brain shuts down to ensure survival - the energy of that experience doesn’t just vanish. It embeds itself in the fascia, the muscles, the nervous system, held as a charge of unprocessed survival energy, a story waiting for a listener who can bear it.

We are conditioned to believe that memory is a function of the hippocampus, a film we can rewind and review, a story with a beginning, middle, and end. Yet, for so many who have navigated the labyrinth of complex trauma, the most striking and disruptive memories have no narrative at all. They are felt, not thought; they are somatic, not cognitive. It’s the inexplicable dread that descends on a perfectly fine Sunday afternoon, a ghost at the feast of an otherwise peaceful day. It’s the sudden, volcanic rage that erupts in response to a minor inconvenience, a reaction so disproportionate it shocks even the one experiencing it. This is the score being kept, not in the ink of words, but in the very architecture of our physical being - a language of sensation that speaks louder and more truthfully than any story the mind could ever construct. The body becomes a living map of the past, and learning to heal is not about erasing that map, but about learning to read its terrain with a deep, abiding, and courageous curiosity.

Neuroception: The Unspoken Sentinel

Long before a single thought forms, a deeper, more ancient part of our intelligence is already at work. The nervous system, through a process Stephen Porges termed neuroception, is constantly scanning the environment - and our internal state ~ for cues of safety or danger. It is the unspoken sentinel at the gate of our experience, deciding whether we can afford to be open, curious, and engaged, or whether we must mobilize for a threat it perceives as imminent. For a system conditioned by trauma, this sentinel is exquisitely sensitive, often misinterpreting neutral cues as dangerous ones.

For those working on vagal tone through breathwork a breathing trainer device (paid link).

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

When the Past is the Present

That becomes the score being played, loudly and without mercy, a symphony of survival that the conscious mind did not compose and cannot stop. The body remembers the precise flavor of helplessness, the icy constriction of terror, the frantic, buzzing energy of a desperate need to flee or fight, or the heavy, leaden collapse of surrender. And it replays that feeling, that entire somatic and emotional state, with devastating fidelity in the present. Understanding this is perhaps the most crucial first step, because it moves the entire experience out of the shame-filled area of being "crazy" or "broken" and into the compassionate, clear-eyed space of biology. The body is simply trying to survive a threat it genuinely believes is happening right now. The path forward, therefore, begins not with fighting the body, but with joining it, acknowledging the flashback for what it is: a memory stored in the body, erupting into the present moment. From that place of recognition, we can begin to learn the life-altering skill of anchoring in the actual present, using our five senses to find irrefutable evidence of safety right here, right now. You can find more on this in our article on grounding techniques that actually work.

The Illusion of a Separate Self

In the midst of this internal chaos, the mind does what it does best: it creates a story. The story is often one of a "broken" self, a "traumatized part," a fundamental flaw at the core of our being. Harris argues that the persistent feeling of a static, unchanging "self" at the center of our experience is, upon closer inspection, an illusion. There is no single, solid entity to be permanently broken.

The Architecture of Returning

If trauma is a process that reorganizes our perception around threat, then healing is the process of consciously reorganizing it around safety, connection, and presence. What emerges is not a return to a "pre-trauma" self, which is an impossible and unhelpful goal. That person is gone, and grieving them is part of the work. It is a movement from a life dictated by the body's unconscious score to a life lived in conscious relationship with it. This process is less about "fixing" and more about "befriending." It is about learning to listen to the body's signals not as pathologies to be silenced, but as communications from a deeply intelligent system that did whatever it had to do to keep us alive.

This reorganization happens on the ground, in the small, unglamorous, and courageous moments of daily life. It happens when we consciously choose to place a hand on our own heart when we feel the familiar, icy clench of anxiety, offering a gesture of warmth and presence to the part of us that is afraid. It happens when we allow ourselves to feel the raw, electric energy of anger without judgment, letting its immense power move through our bodies without needing to aim it at ourselves or another. It is a slow, painstaking, iterative process of building a new kind of internal trust, of proving to the body, one breath and one moment at a time, that the war is finally, truly over. This road is about the courageous movement from a life of dissociation from the neck down to one of deep, authentic embodiment, a topic we explore in our article "What is Embodiment, Really?".

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

From Score to Symphony

For a long time, the body’s score feels like a relentless, discordant noise... a constant hum of anxiety, punctuated by the crashing cymbals of triggers and flashbacks. The work of healing is not to silence the orchestra, but to become its conductor. A conductor holds the entire score, aware of each part, and guides them toward a cohesive, meaningful whole. To conduct our inner orchestra is to hold our own complexity - the parts of us that are terrified, the parts that are resilient, the parts that are angry, the parts that are full of love.

Becoming the conductor means cultivating the radical capacity to be with ourselves, to witness the entire, raging cacophony without becoming lost in it. It is the moment-by-moment practice of presence, of choosing to anchor our attention in the here and now, rather than being swept away by the ghosts of the past or the anxieties of the future. It is the deep, cellular understanding that the body is not betraying us with its symptoms; it is communicating with us in the only language it has ever had. When we finally learn to listen with this kind of receptive attention, the score itself begins to change. The pain, when met with unwavering presence, can transform into a source of real depth, empathy, and compassion for ourselves and for all beings. We learn that we can be both a masterpiece and a work in progress, simultaneously whole and always evolving. The path from trauma to integration is not about erasing the score - an impossible and violent act - but about learning to play it with skill, with heart, and with the full, resonant power of our own reclaimed and embodied presence. It turns out to be the very essence of what it means to move toward genuine post-traumatic growth.

Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. That's not poetry - that's neuroscience. Peter Levine spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the tissues, in the fascia, in the way your muscles hold tension patterns that mirror the exact posture you were in when the worst thing happened.

I've seen this in my own body. The way my shoulders round forward when I feel threatened - that's the fetal position, compressed into a standing posture. The way my jaw clenches before I even know I'm stressed - that's years of swallowed words, swallowed screams, swallowed truth. Your body is an archive. It stores everything.

Here's what I want you to try. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Don't try to change anything. Just notice. Is your breath high and shallow? Is your belly tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Whatever you find, it's okay. You're not doing it wrong. You're just meeting yourself where you are. That's the beginning of everything.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between a regular memory and a trauma memory?

A regular memory is a narrative stored by the brain with a clear sense of time and place; you know it happened in the past. A trauma memory is often stored non-verbally in the body as sensation and emotion, and when triggered, it can feel like the event is happening right now, without the context of it being "over."

Can the body heal even if I can't remember the details of the trauma?

Absolutely. Somatic (body-based) therapies do not require explicit narrative memory to be effective. The focus is on processing the survival energy and sensations stored in the body, allowing the nervous system to complete responses and return to a state of regulation, regardless of whether the mind can construct a story about it.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

I know, I know.

Why do I feel lazy or unmotivated instead of anxious?

What emerges is often a sign of the "freeze" or "shutdown" response in the nervous system. When fighting or fleeing isn't possible, the system can go into a state of collapse or conservation of energy, which can feel like depression, exhaustion, or numbness. It's not a character flaw but a brilliant survival strategy.

How does connecting with others help the body heal?

Humans are wired for connection, and our nervous systems regulate in response to each other - a process called co-regulation. Being in the presence of a calm, safe person can help our own nervous system settle, providing a biological experience of safety that words alone cannot achieve. It turns out to be a core principle of healing that we can learn more about through a consciousness teacher like Kalesh.

The body does keep the score, this much is true. It is an unflinching and brutally honest historian of our lived experience. But it is not a final verdict. The score of trauma does not have to be a life sentence of suffering. What happens when you decide to stop just reading the score, and finally pick up the baton?

A simple tool for working with specific tension points is Lacrosse Ball for Trigger Points (paid link).

What would it mean to stop negotiating with yourself?
